blurb: Colorado's state flower — sky-blue-and-white spurred blooms nodding over ferny foliage.
spread: Clumping & short-lived; reseeds gently to persist
seasons: Blue & white long-spurred flowers late spring–early summer; foliage fades by midsummer
toxic: Mature seeds and roots contain cyanogenic glycosides and are poisonous if eaten in quantity; the flowers were historically nibbled for their sweet nectar in tiny amounts but are not a safe food

care.hardiness: Hardy to at least USDA Zone 3 — fully winter-hardy across the Front Range (Zone 5b–6a). It is native to Colorado's mountains from about 6,000 to 11,500 ft, so cold winters are no problem. The foliage dies back to the ground after frost and re-emerges in early spring. Mulch roots lightly in exposed, wind-swept spots on the open plains.[1]
care.sun: Part shade to full sun, with afternoon protection preferred at lower Front Range elevations. Below about 6,500 ft the intense afternoon sun can scorch the lacy foliage and hasten dormancy — plant on the east side of a structure, under high-branched trees, or with a taller perennial to the west. In cooler mountain gardens or north-facing slopes, full sun is fine. The rule of thumb: the hotter and drier the site, the more afternoon shade it needs.[1]
care.soil: Moist, well-drained soil — it detests both drought and waterlogging. In its native habitat it grows in rocky, humus-rich talus and meadow soils (pH roughly 6.0–7.0). On the plains, amend heavy clay with compost and coarse grit to open it up. Mulch the root zone with 1–2 in. of composted bark or wood chips to keep soil cool and hold moisture — this is especially important on the windy, dry Front Range.[1]
care.water: Moderate; water deeply and consistently, especially during bloom and in midsummer heat. It prefers 1–2 deep waterings per week during hot, dry spells while actively growing; ease off once the foliage begins to yellow and die back in late summer. Avoid overhead watering on the foliage, which encourages mildew. Water at the base.[1]
care.spacing: Space plants 12–18 in. apart; mature clumps are 1–1.5 ft wide and 1–2.5 ft tall. Individual plants are short-lived (3–5 years) but self-seed gently to persist in the garden; allow some seed to set each year.[1]
care.sow: Direct-sow in early fall — seeds naturally cold-stratify over winter and germinate in spring. For spring sowing, refrigerate seeds in moist sand for 60 days (about 2 months) first. Surface-sow or barely cover with a thin layer of soil; seeds need light to germinate. Plants bloom in their second or third year from seed, so plan ahead.[1]
care.stratify: Requires 60 days of cold-moist stratification before spring sowing. Place seed in damp sand or on a moist paper towel in a sealed bag and refrigerate for 2 months. Fall-sown seed stratifies naturally outdoors. Without cold treatment, spring-sown seed germinates poorly and unevenly.[1]
care.depth: Sow on the soil surface and press in firmly, or cover with no more than ¼ in. of fine soil — seeds need light for good germination. Soil temperature of 60–70°F promotes germination in 2–4 weeks. Seedlings emerge slowly; be patient.[1]
care.bloom: Late May through early August on the Front Range — the distinctive long-spurred blue-and-white flowers are showiest in June at lower elevations and extend into July–August at higher elevations. Bloom winds down as heat intensifies in midsummer, and the plant typically goes partly dormant by late August. Deadhead spent flowers to extend bloom slightly and keep the plant tidy.[1]
care.feeding: A light top-dressing of compost in spring is sufficient. Avoid high-nitrogen fertilizers, which produce leafy growth at the expense of flowers. In lean native soils it may need nothing at all.[1]
care.maintenance: Deadhead spent flowers to prolong the season; leave a few to set seed for self-sowing. Cut foliage to the ground in late summer once it yellows — this also removes leafminer-infested leaves. Columbine is short-lived (3–5 years); renew by letting it self-seed rather than dividing (taproots dislike disturbance). Seedlings are usually numerous enough to replace aging crowns naturally.[1]
care.selfsow: Self-seeds modestly to persistently — plants scatter seed in late summer and volunteers appear near parent plants the following spring. The seed freely crosses with other columbine cultivars in the garden, so named selections won't come true from seed.[1]
care.troubles: Columbine leafminer (*Phytomyza aquilegiella*) is the most common Front Range pest: the tiny fly larvae tunnel pale squiggly tracks through the leaves in late spring. Unsightly but rarely fatal — cut off infested leaves and discard (don't compost). Aphids cluster on stems in spring; knock off with a strong water spray. Powdery mildew can appear in hot, humid spells; good airflow and avoiding wet foliage help. Sawfly larvae eat the foliage — pick off by hand. The plant is short-lived by nature, not due to pests; succession from self-seed is the best strategy.[1]
care.companions: Aspen fleabane, wild bergamot, native salvias, Rocky Mountain penstemon, prairie smoke (*Geum triflorum*), and shade-tolerant grasses like tufted hairgrass. Its ferny foliage and nodding flowers suit cottage-style and rock garden plantings.[1]
care.planting: Plant nursery-grown starts in spring (April–May) or early fall (September) — both windows work well on the Front Range. Spring planting lets plants establish through the growing season; fall planting (before mid-September) gives roots several weeks to settle before freeze-up. Space 12–18 in. apart and water in well; mulch lightly to retain moisture. Columbine grows happily in containers — a 10–12 in. pot is sufficient for one plant — but container roots are far more cold-exposed than in-ground roots. In Boulder winters container roots can freeze solid: sink the pot to its rim in a sheltered corner of the garden, wrap it with burlap and insulating mulch, or move it to an unheated garage by late October. An in-ground plant in the same zone needs no protection at all; the container version needs active management to survive winter.[1]
care.propagation: Seed is the easiest and most reliable method, and it is how the species perpetuates itself naturally in the garden (see the `sow`, `stratify`, and `depth` entries for the full seed protocol). Sow in early fall for natural stratification, or cold-stratify in the refrigerator for 60 days before a spring sow; seedlings reach bloom size in year two or three. Division is possible but not ideal: columbine develops a taproot and resents disturbance, so divided plants often sulk or fail. If you need to divide, do it in very early spring before growth begins or in early fall, keeping as much root as possible and replanting immediately. The most sustainable long-term strategy is to let established plants self-seed freely — seedlings appear near the parent each spring and replace aging crowns naturally. Note that garden columbines cross-pollinate freely, so offspring of named cultivars will vary in color.[1]

edible.card: Flowers in tiny amounts (seeds & roots toxic)
edible.summary: Columbine flowers were historically eaten in tiny amounts as a sweet, nectar-flavored garnish, but the mature seeds and roots contain cyanogenic glycosides and are genuinely poisonous — this is not a casual edible.[2]
edible.parts: Only the flowers have any food history: several Indigenous North American peoples sucked nectar from columbine blooms or ate small numbers of flowers with spring greens, and the petals were once eaten in small quantities as a sweet, colorful salad garnish. The sweetness comes from the nectar in the spurs. The seeds and roots are not food.[2]
edible.preparation: If used at all, only a few fresh flowers as a garnish — never the seeds, roots, or leaves, and never in quantity. There is no safe way to prepare the seeds or roots for eating. Given the documented poisonings, the conservative approach on the Front Range is to treat columbine as ornamental and not eat it.[2]
edible.caution: The mature seeds and roots contain cyanogenic glycosides (dhurrin and triglochinin) that are dangerous to the heart if consumed: documented cases include convulsions, respiratory distress, and heart failure after eating fresh foliage, and a child who ate a dozen flowers developed limb weakness, cyanosis, drowsiness, and pinpoint pupils. Keep seeds, roots, and foliage away from children and pets, and do not rely on the old 'flowers are safe' assumption — escalate to caution.[2][3]
